title: Cat Panting with Open Mouth: Is it Stress or an Emergency?
meta_desc: Learn why cats pant with an open mouth, why it's a high-urgency emergency, and how photos help veterinary triage teams save lives.
content_html:
TL;DR: Open-mouth breathing in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency indicating severe respiratory distress. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat is panting, especially if they have blue-tinged gums or are lethargic.

What does it mean when my cat is breathing with its mouth open?

While dogs frequently pant to regulate their body temperature, cats are obligate nasal breathers. If a cat is breathing with an open mouth, it is often a sign of significant physiological distress. This behavior can be triggered by extreme heat, severe stress, or underlying health issues affecting the heart or lungs. It indicates that the cat is struggling to meet its body's oxygen demands.

Is open-mouth panting in cats considered a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Open-mouth breathing or heavy panting in a cat should always be treated as a medical emergency.
  • Even if you suspect the cause is stress, the physical manifestation of panting means the cat's system is under dangerous levels of strain.
  • You must seek immediate veterinary care if the panting is accompanied by blue-tinged gums, lethargy, or a refusal to move.

How can a video of my cat's breathing help with veterinary triage?

  • Capture a quick 5-10 second video of the behavior, as this can be life-saving during a breathing emergency.
  • A video allows the veterinary team to observe the exact character of the breathing—such as abdominal effort or frequency—even if the cat hides symptoms at the clinic.
  • This visual evidence leads to a faster, more accurate diagnosis and immediate stabilization upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in cats manifests as an increased respiratory rate and effort, followed by a change in respiratory pattern. Postural changes may occur where cats sit crouched on all four limbs with the sternum slightly elevated. Labored, open-mouth breathing and changes in mucous membrane color (gray and/or blue [cyanosis]) develop and indicate significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest, indicating an emergency. The location of the pathology, whether pleural space or parenchymal disease, can be determined by careful observation of the breathing pattern and auscultation of the thorax. It is important to differentiate open-mouth breathing due to respiratory distress from other causes, such as stress or oral inflammation.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: Can stress alone cause a cat to pant?
faqs.answer: While extreme stress can cause panting, it is still a sign of physical crisis. It is difficult to distinguish between 'just stress' and a life-threatening respiratory event without a professional exam.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool, minimize noise, and keep your cat in a secure carrier. Avoid hovering over them or causing further stress, as they need as much oxygen as possible.

faqs.question: What does it mean if my cat's tongue is blue?
faqs.answer: Blue or purple-tinged gums and tongue indicate cyanosis, which means your cat is not getting enough oxygen. This is a critical emergency requiring immediate oxygen therapy.
